document:
Spent way too long today trying to repot the fiddle leaf fig. I'd been putting it off for months because I knew it would be annoying. The thing is so tall now it tips over if you even look at it wrong.

Got the soil and a bigger pot and just went for it. Tipped the whole plant on its side to work the roots out of the old pot and immediately dirt went everywhere. All over the floor, on my pants. Of course. The roots were wrapped around the bottom in this tight spiral. I was trying to loosen them but kept thinking I was going to break something important. Don't actually know if you're supposed to be rough with roots or gentle. Probably somewhere in between.

The new pot looked huge compared to the plant itself but apparently that's the point. I filled it with soil and the plant just sat there looking skinny and dramatic. Got some water in it and now I guess I wait.

The whole thing took an hour and a half because I kept second-guessing myself. Had to look things up on my phone twice. Also had to sweep up because Beatriz was coming over later and I didn't want to seem like I just live in a pile of potting soil, which is basically what happened.

Plant's by the window now in its new spot. Looks a little shocked, if plants can look shocked. My back hurts from bending over the whole time. Pretty sure I got dirt under my fingernails that won't come out. Worth it though probably. At least it won't fall over as much.

The squeak started three weeks ago, a small protest from somewhere in the front wheel vicinity. I ignored it the way you ignore a check-engine light, which is to say completely, until it became impossible to ride without feeling like I was dragging a small bird along the sidewalk.

I watched a video on my phone. The man in the video had impressive forearms and spoke with absolute certainty about derailleur cables and brake pads. His workshop was immaculate. He made it look like something you'd want to do, which should have been my first warning.

I bought a multi-tool that came with seventeen different attachments and instructions printed so small I needed glasses I don't have. I flipped the bike upside down as instructed, which made me feel both serious and ridiculous—like I was performing the role of someone competent rather than being that person.

The squeak, it turned out, was coming from neither the wheel nor the brakes but from the brake pad rubbing slightly against the rim. This required loosening one small bolt, which took me forty minutes. The bolt was tight. Then it was too loose. Then I couldn't find the right tension, so I called Marisol, who came over and turned it a quarter inch and the squeak disappeared.

"You just had to feel it," she said, handing back the wrench.

I thought about all the things that require feeling rather than knowing, the ones you can't really watch videos about. The bike is quiet now. I still have the multi-tool. I'm less confident than I was before I started, which I suppose means I actually learned something. The squeak

Spent way too long today fighting with the rubber plant. It's been looking sad for months and I finally committed to repotting it, which sounds simple but honestly turned into this whole thing.

The pot was completely rootbound when I got it out—like a solid mass of roots wrapped around itself. Had to soak the whole thing in water for like twenty minutes just to loosen it up enough to work with. My hands got all pruney and there was potting soil everywhere. The sink was a disaster.

The new pot is only slightly bigger because I didn't want to overdo it, but getting the plant to actually sit in there was ridiculous. The root ball kept tilting sideways no matter how I positioned it. I'd get it looking okay and then it would just... fall over. Added more soil around the edges but it still looked wonky. At one point I considered just giving up and putting it back in the old pot, which would've been stupid but I was genuinely frustrated.

Eventually I wedged it in there with soil packed pretty firmly and it's staying upright now, at least. The whole process took like an hour when it should've taken fifteen minutes. Water got splashed on my pants. There are little bits of bark scattered across the kitchen floor that I keep finding.

Anyway, it's done. The plant looks a bit traumatized but hopefully it'll actually start growing now instead of just existing sadly in the corner. I'll probably water it tomorrow even though I shouldn't, because I always do that. We'll see if it survives my

The garage has become impossible. What started as a temporary spot for the broken shelf and some paint cans has somehow absorbed everything else — old sports equipment, three toolboxes, a defunct printer, several boxes of things you meant to sort through, tangled extension cords, half-empty bags of fertilizer, and a bicycle that hasn't been ridden in years.

The usual approach — clearing everything out into the driveway and starting fresh — isn't realistic when you've got neighbours and limited time. Better to work in sections.

Start with one wall or corner. Pull everything out, get a sense of what's actually there. Most of it will surprise you. You'll find tools you forgot you owned and things that can simply be thrown out. The broken lamp has been taking up space for two years. The box of instruction manuals for appliances you no longer have serves no purpose. Be ruthless about this part.

Once you've sorted, group like items together. Tools in one area. Seasonal stuff in another. Sports things grouped up. This immediately makes the space feel more manageable and means you can actually find things later instead of just buying new ones because you can't locate the existing ones.

Wall space is your friend here. A pegboard, some simple shelving, or even just hooks on the walls will transform things. Get things off the floor where possible.

Noor mentioned she uses clear plastic bins for anything that needs to stay but isn't immediately useful. You can see what's in them without opening them up. That matters more than you'd think when you're looking for something specific.

The whole process takes a weekend if you're actually working. Maybe longer if you're doing it in short bursts, which is more realistic for most people.

The afternoon had settled into that particular kind of grey that makes the light seem to come from nowhere specific. Through the kitchen window, the rain continued its steady percussion against the glass, and the forecast suggested it wouldn't stop anytime soon. By two o'clock, there wasn't much alternative to staying inside.

Petra had suggested the board games sometime around lunch, and no one had objected. The box came down from the shelf where it had been sitting since late autumn—the cardboard soft at the edges, the components still mostly intact. We spread the board across the coffee table, which required moving the usual accumulation of magazines and mugs. The rulebook had that familiar problem of being simultaneously too detailed and unclear about specific edge cases.

The first game moved slowly. Felix kept second-guessing his strategy, which slowed the pace but also meant fewer quick defeats. Mateo played with the kind of distracted focus that suggested he was only partially present, occasionally glancing at his phone before catching himself and returning to the board. It didn't feel like the intention was particularly competitive.

By the third game, something had shifted. We'd stopped explaining every rule and simply played, pointing to the board when questions arose. The rain had become background noise. Someone had put the kettle on twice. The afternoon had taken on that suspended quality where time feels less urgent, where it's acceptable to spend hours on something that has no external purpose.

Around five o'clock, when the light had deteriorated further and the rain showed no signs of stopping, we'd stopped keeping score entirely. The game continued mostly out of momentum.

So today I finally dealt with that awful squeaking noise on my bike. It's been driving me absolutely mental for weeks now. Every time I rode it, this high-pitched squeak that seemed to come from nowhere in particular. I kept meaning to just take it to the shop but honestly I was too annoyed to deal with looking up their hours and waiting around.

I watched a couple of videos online this morning and figured I'd just try it myself. Turns out most of it is super obvious once someone shows you. The thing was my brake pads rubbing slightly on the rim. I couldn't actually see it at first—had to spin the wheel while the bike was upside down on the grass to watch where it was catching. Then I just loosened the bolts, adjusted the position slightly so there was a tiny gap, and tightened everything back.

I was genuinely shocked it worked. Like, I felt properly pleased with myself for maybe ten minutes, which is embarrassing but also kind of nice? Tomas rang while I was doing it and I almost didn't answer because I was so focused, which never happens with me.

The squeak's completely gone now. I took it around the block twice just to make sure. It's funny how something that small—literally millimetres of rubber rubbing on metal—can make such a difference to how the whole bike feels. Makes me think I should probably look at other stuff while I'm at it. The chain could probably use attention. But maybe I'll just ride it like this for a bit first and enjoy the peace and quiet. It's nice having something just work properly for once.

There's something about the ritual of making coffee that keeps drawing me back, even when instant would be faster. I've settled into three methods that each feel right for different moments.

The pour-over is what I reach for most mornings. You need a filter, grounds, and hot water—nothing fancy required. I heat the water until it's just off boiling, then wet the filter first to rinse it and warm the cup. The grounds go in, and you pour slowly in circles, watching the coffee bloom and darken. It takes maybe four minutes, and there's something meditative about that attention, that restraint. You can't rush it, which is partly the point.

Then there's the stovetop pot, the old metal kind. It's quicker once you've got it going, but it demands respect—easy to scorch the grounds if you're not careful. The coffee comes out stronger, almost aggressive compared to the pour-over's clarity. I use it less often, mostly when Dmitri comes by because he likes it that way, thick and insistent.

The third is just French press when I have time to sit. Coarse grounds, hot water, four minutes steep, then the slow push of the plunger. It's the least refined method technically, all those particles settling in your cup, but there's an honesty to it. You taste everything.

What strikes me now is how little has really changed in how I approach this. I've tried different ratios, different waters, different temperatures, but I keep returning to these three. They're not optimized. They're just—real, I suppose. The coffee tastes like coffee. It takes time. And that seems enough.

So we went out Saturday morning thinking we'd do this easy loop trail that's supposed to take about an hour and a half. The weather was nice, we had water, snacks, the whole thing. I'd been on my phone the night before looking at the route and it seemed straightforward enough.

We started out fine, made it through the first part where the trail is really clear and marked. But then we got to this junction and I think that's where we messed up. There were two paths and the one we took looked more worn, which we figured meant more people go that way, so it must be the right one. Turns out we were wrong about that.

For maybe twenty minutes we just kept going because we thought the trail would start looking familiar, or we'd hit a landmark that made sense. But it kept getting quieter and less maintained. Owen wanted to turn back earlier but we kept saying oh we're almost there, it's fine. Classic mistake, I guess.

Eventually we had to admit we were lost and pulled out the map. Turns out we'd gone way off course—not dangerously so, but we'd added probably forty-five minutes to the whole thing and gone in a direction that wasn't anywhere on the route we planned. Nothing dramatic happened. We just turned around, backtracked to the junction we remembered, and took the other path.

When we finally got back to the car we were annoyed with ourselves but also kind of laughing about it. The whole thing took almost three hours instead of ninety minutes. My feet hurt and I was thirsty. But yeah, it's one of those things that happens. You think you know where you're going and then you don't

How to Reorganise Your Garage

The first thing you'll want to do is accept that this will take longer than you think. Tell yourself it's a Saturday project. It will consume two weekends and a Tuesday evening when you can't sleep.

Start by removing everything. Yes, everything. Drag it onto the driveway or the lawn. This serves two purposes: you'll actually see what you own (depressing) and you'll have a clean floor to work with. Don't get sentimental yet. That comes later.

Sort into piles. Keep, donate, sell, and trash are the traditional categories, though most people end up with a fifth pile called "mysterious" — the things you genuinely can't identify or remember acquiring. Ask Hana if she knows what that blue contraption is. She won't.

Now examine the walls. Those pegboards everyone buys but never installs? This is your moment. Or bins. Everyone needs bins. Buy more than seems reasonable; you'll still run out.

Before putting anything back, do one practical thing like sweep. Or hose it down if you're feeling ambitious, though honestly most people don't. The floor will still be cleaner than before.

Put frequently used items at eye level and within reach. Tools should go somewhere that makes sense, even if that sense only exists in your head. The seasonal decorations can live in the back corner where they belong.

Accept that it won't stay organised. Grit and entropy are powerful forces. Six months from now there will be a cardboard box near the door containing items no one will claim. That's fine. At least you'll know where to find the ladder.

Started the dough around half past six, though I wasn't fully awake yet. Mixed the flour and water first—630 grams flour to 420 water, which is a reasonable hydration level for the kind of open crumb I'm after. Let it sit for thirty minutes while I made coffee and checked the house was reasonably presentable before the day proper got going.

The autolyse period helped. When I came back to it, the dough felt more extensible, less sticky to handle. Added the salt and worked it in gradually, about 10 grams dissolved in a small amount of water. The whole incorporation process took longer than I'd anticipated—maybe ten minutes of mixing before it felt properly integrated. The dough temperature came to around 26 degrees, which is fine for a bulk fermentation of about four hours.

Luca wanted breakfast while I was halfway through the stretch-and-fold routine at the two-hour mark. I managed the first set of folds one-handed, which wasn't ideal but functional. The dough showed good gluten development—there was visible resistance but also elasticity. Left it in the bowl with a damp cloth over the top.

The kitchen has warmed up by midmorning, which will probably speed things along. I'm aiming for the dough to show a 50 percent volume increase before shaping, though honestly it's difficult to judge precisely in a covered bowl. The surface looks slightly domed and there are some visible bubbles around the edges where it contacts the glass. 

Still need to shape it, cold proof overnight, and score before baking tomorrow. The whole timeline depends on how this afternoon's fermentation actually progresses. These things never go entirely to plan, but the early stages seem reasonable so

I spent most of Saturday in the garage, which is to say I spent most of Saturday moving things from one corner to another and then moving them back again.

The usual suspects were there. Boxes I haven't opened in four years, their contents unknown and possibly unknowable by now. A broken lamp that I've been meaning to fix—or throw away, more honestly. Paint cans from the kitchen renovation, three-quarters empty, their lids crusted shut. Sanjay's old bicycle that he keeps saying he'll collect one of these days. Tools scattered across the workbench like someone had exploded mid-project, which they had, sometime last April.

What struck me, moving things around, was how much of it is just... waiting. Not useful, not broken enough to discard without guilt, just waiting in the dark for some future version of me to deal with it properly. I found myself reading labels on old boxes, remembering what I'd thought I might need to keep. A lot of it seemed important at the time.

I made three piles: things to actually use, things to donate, and things I simply couldn't decide about. That third pile is still there. It's the pile that defeats me every time—not because I'm sentimental, but because decision-making feels harder than just leaving things be. Moving something to a shelf where I can see it, organizing it into a neat stack, that feels like progress. Actually committing to getting rid of it feels permanent in a way I'm not ready for.

By evening the garage looked better. Cleaner, anyway. More intentional. But I know by next month it'll have drifted back into comfortable chaos. There's something almost honest about that—about having a space that reflects how things actually accumulate in life, haphazardly and without

I spent most of Saturday in the garage, which sounds productive until you understand that I mainly just stood there staring at things. The bike I bought three years ago and rode twice. A shelf unit that's been leaning against the wall since we moved. Boxes of old textbooks that I keep telling myself I'll look through someday but won't.

My partner kept walking past and asking if I wanted help, which was nice but also made me feel worse about the fact that nothing was actually happening. I'd move one thing and then lose momentum, distracted by finding something else I'd forgotten about—a broken lamp, some old kitchen scales, a tennis racket with a split in the frame.

By afternoon I'd at least created zones. Tools in one corner. Seasonal stuff by the door. The broken things got their own awkward pile that I'm pretty sure will just stay there until someone else gets fed up and throws them out. I managed to stack the boxes more neatly, and that felt like something, even though they're still boxes of textbooks taking up space.

The worst part is how quickly it gets messy again. Three weeks and I bet there'll be new things in here, tools left out from whatever project got started, maybe someone's old chair that we're "temporarily" storing. I know this because it's happened every other time I've done this.

Still, there's something about being able to see the actual floor, about knowing where the screwdrivers are. It's not finished—nothing feels finished—but it's better than Saturday morning, which is probably the most honest thing I can say about it.

The rain started around ten in the morning, the kind that doesn't let up. My partner suggested board games before noon, which would have annoyed me on any other day—that forced cheerfulness about making the best of things. But the gutters were already loud enough that thinking felt pointless, so I said okay.

We pulled out the box we'd had sitting on a shelf since someone gave it to us at Christmas. The board was this cardboard thing that smelled faintly chemical when we unfolded it. The rules were dense and half the tokens were missing, replaced by buttons and a dime. We read through the instructions twice and still weren't entirely sure what we were doing.

It didn't matter much. We played badly, laughing when we miscounted spaces or forgot which piece was supposed to do what. My partner kept making a voice for their character. We had tea that went cold on the side table. At one point Ruth came home from work early because of the weather and just kind of watched us for a while, then sat down and played the last game with us without asking questions.

The thing about a rainy afternoon is that time feels different. There's no sense you should be doing something else. You're not wasting anything because what else would you be doing anyway? The whole day is already written off. You can just sit there moving pieces around a board while water hits the windows and nobody minds that you're not winning or even playing correctly.

We never did finish. Ruth left for something, and eventually the rain eased up enough that we put the box away. But that gap between ten and four felt like something we'd actually done, rather than just survived.

There's something almost meditative about the different ways I've learned to make coffee, each one revealing something small about how I was living at that particular moment.

The first method was the machine—automatic, mindless, perfect for those years when I barely had time to think. You fill the reservoir, add grounds to the filter, press the button, and walk away. The coffee was always there when you needed it, hot and competent and utterly forgettable. I remember standing in the kitchen before work, not really tasting it, just drinking it for the caffeine and the warm cup in my hands. There was something honest about that, actually. The machine asked nothing of me except that I remember to buy more filters.

Then came the pour-over phase. Ingrid brought one to a dinner party and made it seem like the simplest, most elegant thing. The equipment fit in a small box: the dripper, a paper filter, a gooseneck kettle. Suddenly there was a ritual involved. You had to wait for the water to cool slightly, you had to pour slowly in circles, you had to pay attention for those three or four minutes. It felt like a small act of care directed at myself. I'd stand there in the morning quiet, watching the dark liquid drip through, and it was almost meditative. Some days I'd do it twice just to have that moment.

The French press came later, or maybe I just returned to it. I'm not sure anymore. With that one, you're not trying to refine the experience—you're just steeping the grounds directly in hot water for four minutes, then pressing down the plunger. The coffee tastes heavier, less filtered, more itself somehow. You can taste the oils, the sediment, the parts that the paper filters always caught. It feels less fussy than the pour-over, more honest than the machine. There's less room for pretense.

What's strange is that I don't think any of these ways is actually better than the others. The machine still works fine on mornings when I'm rushing. The pour-over still creates that small moment of attention when I need one. The press pot is there when I want something less refined.

I think what matters is that you notice which one you're using, and why. Because for a while, I wasn't noticing anything at all.

Subject: Squeaky rear brake — finally figured it out

So I've had this annoying squeak coming from my rear brake for months now. Every time I brake it's this high-pitched noise that's honestly embarrassing on the bike path. I kept putting it off because I thought I'd need to take it to the shop, but yesterday I finally just tried to fix it myself.

Turned out to be pretty straightforward once I actually looked at it. The brake pads were glazed — they had this shiny coating on them from friction. Apparently that's why they squeaked. I just roughed up the surface of both pads with some sandpaper (maybe 80-grit or so) and the noise went away completely. Took maybe 20 minutes.

Obviously first thing I did was check if the pads were even worn down enough to keep using. Mine still had plenty of material so I didn't need to replace them yet. That was lucky because new pads would've cost more and required actual installation.

The other thing I learned while I had the wheel off — make sure your brake alignment is decent. If the pads aren't hitting the rim straight, you get all kinds of noise. I had to loosen the brake bolts slightly and adjust the angle. Nothing dramatic, just small tweaks until both pads made contact at the same time.

A couple things I wish I'd known beforehand: you need decent light to see what you're doing. I was squinting under my kitchen light for ages before I moved the bike outside. Also, don't just sand the pads if the rim is really dirty. I cleaned the rim with a dry cloth first, which probably helped more than anything else.

The squeak came back once after a couple weeks but much quieter, and after another light sanding it's been fine for a month now. My guess is that's just normal and I'll need to do this again eventually. 

Anyway, if anyone else has been dealing with brake noise, it's worth trying this before spending money at the shop. Just make sure you're comfortable working on your bike and take your time with the alignment. Rushing that part is probably where things go wrong.

## The Philodendron Refuses to Cooperate

There comes a moment in every plant parent's life when you have to accept that your green friend has become a root-bound tyrant, and you're going to have to do something about it. This moment arrived for me on a Tuesday afternoon, standing in my kitchen with a philodendron that had spent six months slowly suffocating itself in a pot the size of a teacup.

I knew it was time. The plant knew it was time. Everyone who visited knew it was time. Yet here we were, locked in a standoff, because repotting is genuinely one of the least intuitive tasks anyone could ask a person to perform with their hands.

The plan seemed simple enough: get a bigger pot, some fresh soil, gently ease the plant out, arrange it nicely, water it. In theory, this takes maybe twenty minutes. In practice, you end up sitting on your kitchen floor at four in the afternoon, wondering if you've somehow chosen the most structurally sound plant in existence.

It wouldn't budge. I ran a knife around the inside of the pot like some kind of deranged surgeon. I tipped it sideways. I used actual force, which you're apparently not supposed to do but which sometimes seems necessary when you're locked in mortal combat with a ceramic vessel. The soil had compacted into something resembling concrete. The roots had fused with the pot itself through sheer spite and determination.

When it finally came free, it came free *violently*, in a way that sent soil flying across my countertop and made me question whether I'd actually been helping or just engaged in plant assault. The root ball was, unsurprisingly, a horrifying tangle of roots circling in on themselves like some botanical ouroboros.

Three new pots and two significant scratches later, it was finally in a home big enough for the next six months of growth. The plant accepted this arrangement with the grace of a teenager being told to clean their room: resigned, unceremonious, already planning to make things difficult again in the near future.

It's sitting by the window now, looking vaguely less distressed than it did an hour ago. Whether it actually needed this or I just needed to feel like I was doing something productive on a Tuesday is anyone's guess. Next time I'll probably wait even longer before attempting it again, which is somehow both the most reasonable decision and the worst one I could possibly make.

Subject: Squeaky bike – where's it coming from?

So my commute bike has developed this annoying squeak and I finally got round to trying to track it down instead of just ignoring it. Took me ages to figure out where the noise was actually coming from because you'd think it'd be obvious but it really isn't.

First thing I did was ride it round the yard and tried to pinpoint which part of the pedal stroke it happened on – turns out it's pretty much constant, just gets louder when you're going slower. That ruled out the pedals themselves which was my first guess.

Started with the chain. Cleaned it properly, which it needed anyway, then lubricated it. Still squeaking. Then I got paranoid it was the bottom bracket so I was poking around under there trying to figure out if anything looked wrong, but it all looked fine to me. Wasn't making any grinding noise either.

The breakthrough came when Ingrid suggested it might be the saddle or seatpost. Felt stupid I hadn't thought of that. Took the seat off, cleaned the post, wiped it down, applied some lube where it slides into the tube. Put it back on, took it for a spin. Nothing. Still squeaking.

By this point I was ready to just accept it as background noise, but I went back and started methodically checking every bolt on the frame – handlebars, brake calipers, the works. Nothing seemed loose but I tightened a few things anyway. Rode it round again. Still there.

Then I realized I hadn't actually checked if it was coming from the front or back. Rode it with one hand and gripped each wheel in turn while moving. Definitely the front. So either the fork or the front brake or the wheel itself.

Spun the front wheel off the ground and that's when I spotted it – the brake pads were sitting slightly rubbing against the rim as the wheel turned. Not hard, just enough to make that consistent squeak. I adjusted the brake caliper and it's gone. Took me about two hours and feeling like an idiot but the noise is finally

The squeak started three weeks ago, thin and persistent, coming from somewhere near the pedals. I ignored it for a while—the bike still worked fine, and I had other things to worry about. But it got worse, turning into something between a squeak and a creak, and eventually I decided I'd rather just fix it than keep listening to it.

I found a video online showing someone taking apart a crankset. It looked straightforward enough in their garage, all bright lighting and organized tools laid out on a cloth. Mine looked different. I had a wrench that was close to the right size, a screwdriver, and a bicycle pump. I pulled the bike into the kitchen where the light was better.

The first bolt came off easily. The second one stuck. I applied more pressure, and it moved, and I kept turning. When I finally got it free, I realized I'd probably been straining against the wrench the wrong way the whole time. A lot of what I was doing seemed to involve figuring out which direction things were actually supposed to turn.

The crank came off. Inside the bottom bracket, where the pedal arms connect to the frame, everything was fairly gunked up with old grease and what looked like dust. I wiped it out with a cloth. This was the part that seemed to be squeaking—when I moved the pedal arms by hand without the crank installed, the noise was definitely coming from here.

I drove to the hardware store and got some general-purpose lubricant because I didn't want to overthink it. Back home, I sprayed some into the bracket, worked the pedal arms back and forth, wiped away the excess. Then I put the crank back on. This part was harder than taking it off—the bolt threads seemed finicky—but I got it tight enough that everything felt solid.

I took the bike out for a test ride around the block. The squeak was gone. There's something satisfying about that, about a problem you can actually locate and fix with basic tools and a little time. The bike is still the same bike, but now it's quieter, and I know where the noise was coming from. If it happens again, I'll know what to do. Though probably I'll wait a while first, until it gets annoying enough that I can't stand it anymore.

We ended up playing board games all afternoon because of the rain. I wasn't really in the mood at first—just wanted to sit around and maybe read or something—but Noor kept pestering me about it and eventually I just gave in. She'd pulled out three different boxes from the closet and laid them all on the coffee table like she was presenting options at a store.

We started with the word one, the one that takes forever. Dmitri arrived around two and immediately complained that he was hungry, so we had to stop and make sandwiches. While we were in the kitchen he told us about some argument he had at work, something about whose turn it was to clean the break room. It sounded stupid but also kind of funny the way he described it. By the time we got back to the game, we'd kind of lost the thread of what we were even doing, so we just packed that one away.

The second game was actually better. It's the kind where you're trying to build something and also occasionally screw over the other players. Noor was winning for a while, which made her unbearable—she kept doing this annoying laugh every time she got ahead. Then Dmitri came back and totally flipped the board around. I mean, not literally, but he had a run of luck that was kind of insane. I mostly just sat there and tried not to fall asleep because the living room was warm and the rain kept hitting the windows and it was all very cozy in a way that made me want to just lie down.

We played until it was dark out, which seemed to happen earlier than it should have. My back hurt from sitting in that one chair too long. Noor ordered food from somewhere because nobody wanted to cook dinner, and we just kept playing while we waited. The last game was shorter. Nobody was really paying full attention anymore.

The rain didn't stop. If anything it got heavier. By the time Dmitri left, it was coming down hard enough that you couldn't really see across the street. Noor wanted to keep playing but I was honestly tired of it by then, so we just put everything back in the closet. She went to bed early and I ended up watching something on the screen for a while before I got tired enough to sleep.

Anyone else just completely overthink their morning coffee routine or is it just me?

So I've been going back and forth between three methods lately and honestly I can't decide. I used to be a drip person my whole life — just throw the grounds in, add water, wait five minutes. Super mindless which I kind of liked when I was still half asleep. But then my partner got this pour-over thing and insisted I try it. I was skeptical because it seemed needlessly fussy, but there's something almost meditative about it? You've got to watch the water temperature, pour slowly, let it bloom for a minute. Takes like eight minutes total but the coffee tastes noticeably different. Smoother somehow.

The problem is I don't always have eight minutes. That's where the French press comes in. I got one of those a few months ago and it's become my weekday go-to. Heat water, throw grounds in, wait four minutes, plunge. Done. It's a bit stronger than the pour-over and you definitely get more sediment at the bottom, which some people hate but honestly I don't mind. Sometimes that's the good stuff.

Then there's the moka pot situation. My mum has always used one and kept insisting I needed to understand it. I finally borrowed hers to see what the deal was. It's not as hard as people make it sound but there's definitely a learning curve — you have to pay attention to the heat or it gets bitter. When you nail it though? Really intense, almost espresso-like. Good for when you want to actually taste *something* in your coffee, you know?

I think my issue is I'm trying to use all three and my kitchen counter is getting ridiculous. There's no room for anything else. Ingrid said I should just pick one and stop being indecisive, which is fair, but each one is so different depending on my mood that morning.

I guess the real question is whether anyone actually sticks with just one method long-term or if everyone ends up with multiple coffee devices slowly taking over their kitchen. Because I'm starting to wonder if this is just what happens once you care enough to notice the difference between them. You can't unknow it.

What's everyone else doing? Am I the only person with this problem or

The trail marker was supposed to be at the fork. We'd looked at the map—or rather, I'd looked at the map while the others stood around—and it seemed straightforward enough. Left at the fork, follow the ridge, and we'd loop back to the parking area by late afternoon.

We went right instead.

I don't think any of us noticed immediately. The path was clear enough, tree-lined, and it felt like we were moving through landscape that could have been the correct landscape. Mateo was talking about something from work. Ruth was behind us somewhere, taking photographs of moss. The forest looks the same in all directions when you're inside it, which is something I'd never really understood until Saturday.

It was maybe forty minutes before someone asked if the trail seemed different than expected. By then we'd descended further than the map suggested we should have, and the ridge we were supposed to follow had become something else—a ravine, maybe, or just the general falling-away of the land. The sun was still high. No one was panicked. But there was that particular quiet that settles in when you realize something has shifted.

We stopped and looked at the map again. The path we were on wasn't marked. Or it was marked, but as something minor, something that connected to other things we hadn't planned to connect to. The kind of detail you skip over when you're planning a two-hour loop.

What I remember most clearly is the conversation about whether to turn back or keep going. Mateo thought we should backtrack. Ruth pointed out that we could probably reach the main road if we followed the ravine down, and from there it was just a matter of walking along the shoulder. It was sensible, which is maybe why we did it. We'd already walked this far. The afternoon was still young.

It took another hour and a half, and the road, when we reached it, was farther from civilization than we'd expected. We waited in the parking area of a closed restaurant for someone to pick us up. Our legs ached. My feet had started to blister in places I didn't know could blister.

Mateo laughed at some point and said the walk had been longer than intended. Ruth agreed. I remember thinking that it hadn't been bad, exactly—nothing had gone wrong in any important way—but something about the inadvertency of it bothered me. We'd simply walked the wrong path and adjusted. And that felt like the realest thing about it

The walking routes through our neighbourhood have contracted over time, though not in any formal way. There are no official closures, no diverted signage. Rather, the paths have simply narrowed in the collective consciousness—certain stretches avoided, others favoured to the point where the grass grows thin along the preferred line.

The old route to the station used to cut through the residential streets behind the medical centre, a modest detour that passed three parks and a small library. Most people still remember it, though fewer walk it now. The library closed four years ago. One park underwent renovation and remains fenced. The other two parks are accessible, but the connecting paths between them require navigating a section where the pavement has buckled—nothing dangerous, but enough of an inconvenience that most people have switched to the main road. The main road is direct but considerably less pleasant: car fumes, no shade, the monotonous rhythm of traffic lights.

What interests me about this isn't the decline itself but the strange persistence of the old knowledge. People still mention the route. Someone's visiting aunt still insists on taking it, claiming the air is better. Yuki once remarked that they remembered blackcurrant bushes somewhere along the second park, though neither of us could locate them on our last attempt. The route exists now primarily in conversation—a kind of phantom geography that shapes how we think about the neighbourhood's layout, even as we move through it along different lines.

There's a newer route, or perhaps I should say a rediscovered one. Behind the row of converted warehouses, there's a path that connects to a wider system of walkways that most people had simply forgotten about. The warehouses were renovated three years ago, and the path became more visible once the site was no longer obscured by temporary fencing. It's pleasant enough—industrial in character, with occasional glimpses of the canal. But it requires knowing to look for it. The signage is minimal. I suspect it will either become established as a regular route, or it will fade back into obscurity as the attention that briefly illuminated it moves elsewhere.

This is how neighbourhoods actually work, I think. Not through grand transformations but through the gradual reweighting of small decisions. Each person choosing the main road slightly more often, or discovering the path by the warehouses. Nothing tragic, nothing triumphant. Just the slow redrawing of how we move through shared space.

There's a plant on my kitchen counter that's been asking for a new pot for about three months now. I can tell because the roots have started circling the drainage hole like they're looking for a way out, and the soil dries out in what feels like minutes after watering. Every time I water it, some spills onto the floor because there's nowhere for the water to go. I've walked past it countless times thinking, "Today's the day," and then I just... don't.

I'm not sure what makes me hesitant. It's not complicated—I have a bigger pot, I have soil, I have newspaper to spread out and contain the mess. But there's something about the actual doing of it that keeps getting pushed to tomorrow. Maybe it's the knowledge that my living room will briefly look like a disaster. Maybe it's the small violence of pulling something out of where it's been comfortable, even if that comfort has run its course.

Kofi came over last week and looked at the plant without saying anything, which somehow made me feel worse about the whole situation. Then he asked if I wanted help, which was generous, but also made it more real—made it something that needed doing rather than something I could keep indefinitely in the planning stage.

This morning I finally did it. I cleared the counter, laid down more newspaper than necessary, and worked the pot free. The plant resisted a little—roots had gripped the sides—and I felt oddly guilty, like I was being too rough. But once it came loose, it was fine. The roots were healthy and pale and numerous. I trimmed back the ones that had gotten too aggressive, set it in the new pot with fresh soil, watered it carefully, and that was that. Twenty minutes, maybe less.

Now it's sitting in its new home and looks somehow both the same and different. More room to grow, though it doesn't know that yet. It's still adjusting, still the same plant, but with more possibility in front of it.

The strange thing is how relieved I feel, and how little that relief has to do with the plant itself. It's something about finally crossing over from thinking about doing something to actually having done it. The weight of that small postponement is lighter now that it's gone.

Making Bread from Scratch: A Morning Process

The timing depends largely on your schedule and ambient temperature. Most people find that starting the night before allows the dough to develop flavor through a long, slow fermentation. Mix flour, water, salt, and a small amount of yeast in the evening—perhaps around 8 p.m.—then let it sit in a covered bowl at room temperature until morning.

By the time you wake up, the dough should have roughly doubled in volume and developed a slightly sour smell, which indicates fermentation is underway. The dough will feel puffy and somewhat slack. This is normal and expected.

Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured work surface. It doesn't need to be kneaded aggressively at this point; instead, perform what's called a fold. Stretch one edge toward the center, rotate the bowl slightly, and repeat this motion around the entire mass three or four times. This takes maybe a minute and strengthens the gluten structure. Return the dough to the bowl.

Let it rest for thirty minutes, covered with a damp cloth to prevent a skin from forming. Meanwhile, you might prepare other things: check that your oven rack is positioned correctly, prepare your baking vessel (a Dutch oven works well), and ensure you have enough counter space for shaping.

After the rest period, perform another round of folds using the same technique. Then allow another thirty-minute rest. Repeat this process twice more, spacing each round by thirty minutes. This is called the bulk fermentation phase, and it typically takes two to two-and-a-half hours total. The dough should increase in volume noticeably and feel aerated.

When ready to shape, flour your work surface again and turn the dough out. Working gently to avoid deflating all the gas, fold the dough into a round ball by pulling the edges toward the center and rotating as you work. Flip it seam-side-down on your work surface.

If you have a banneton basket or a bowl lined with a floured cloth, transfer the shaped dough seam-side-up into it. Otherwise, a bowl works fine. Cover it and let it undergo final proof for one to four hours, depending on room temperature. Colder environments require longer times.

Toward the end of the proof, preheat your oven to 450 degrees with the Dutch oven inside it. When the dough appears puffy and springs back slowly when poked (not immediately, not not at all), it's ready.

Transfer the dough carefully into the hot Dutch oven, score the top with a sharp blade, cover with the lid, and bake for twenty minutes covered, then thirty minutes uncovered until deep golden brown.

The entire process from mixing to finished loaf takes approximately eighteen to twenty hours, though active work time amounts to only thirty or forty minutes spread throughout the morning.

So this happened last Saturday and I'm still kind of annoyed about it lol

We decided to do this hike that supposedly takes about 2 hours, nothing crazy, just wanted to get outside for a bit. The weather was perfect so we grabbed our stuff and headed out. I had printed out directions from one of those hiking websites and everything seemed straightforward enough.

First hour went fine, we were on the main trail, seeing other people, all good. Then we get to this junction and I swear the sign was pointing us the right way but honestly now I'm not even sure. We took what we thought was a left but maybe it was more of a straight? I don't know, the path looked legit at least.

After like 20 minutes we started noticing the trail was getting way less maintained. Like there were still definitely footprints so people had been through there, but it was narrower and there were more roots and fallen branches. We kept going though because we figured the trail had to loop back or connect somewhere. That was dumb.

This went on for another 40 minutes probably, and then Kofi was like "I don't think this is right" and we stopped to look at my printout again. The problem is I'd only printed out the main section of the trail map so there was basically nothing showing where we actually were at that point. And obviously my phone had no signal.

We ended up just retracing our steps which took forever because now we had to backtrack the whole thing. It was getting late and we were all tired and honestly kind of frustrated with each other which is always fun. We made it back to the main trail eventually and took a different route down that was much clearer.

In the end we were out there like 4.5 hours instead of 2. My legs felt it the next day. The whole thing is kind of funny in retrospect but in the moment I was legitimately stressed about whether we'd have to call someone.

Anyway moral of the story I guess is actually look at your map instead of just assuming and maybe don't go hiking with only a printed screenshot. Also the website that had the original trail info was not super helpful about what the junction actually looks like so I'd avoid that one.

Just wanted to vent I guess. Has anyone else had a trail go sideways like this?

The thing about hiking is that you can be absolutely certain you're on the right trail for maybe the first twenty minutes, and then doubt creeps in. That's what happened to us that Saturday.

We'd printed the map from online—the kind with the little squiggly lines and the blue stream marked alongside what was supposed to be the main path. Three of us: me, Noor, and Petra. We started around nine, which felt early enough, though it never is. The weather was decent. We weren't attempting anything difficult, just a loop that someone had said would take about two hours, maybe two and a half.

The first part was textbook. There was the parking area, the gate, the wooden signpost with faded lettering. We took a photo like people do. After that, the trail was obvious—well-worn, other people had been through recently. We could see boot prints in the softer sections.

But about forty minutes in, at a spot where the path seemed to widen and fork without much warning, we stopped. There was no sign. The map suggested we should keep left toward the stream, but what we could see of the trail going left looked less used, thinner. The right fork looked more traveled. We stood there maybe five minutes, which is a long time when you're trying to make a decision. Noor said left, Petra wanted to follow the busier path, and I was the person holding the map, so naturally I was responsible for the whole thing. I suggested we go right for a bit and see if it looped back.

It didn't loop back. Instead we ended up on what was essentially a deer trail, steeper than anticipated, winding up instead of across the landscape. We could still see the stream below us, which was something—at least we weren't totally lost in featureless forest. And none of us were panicking yet, just quietly annoyed. That's the tricky part of hiking mistakes. Nobody wants to be the person who admits it was probably wrong to go this way.

We backtracked after maybe fifteen minutes of the uphill climb. It took longer to get back to the fork than it should have. By then our legs were tired in a different way than expected, and we were quieter with each other. The left path turned out to be the right one, and once we were on it, everything made sense again. We saw where we'd gone wrong—there was even a small cairn we'd somehow missed.

We finished the loop, but the timing was off. The hike that was supposed to be two and a half hours took nearly four. Nobody said much about it afterward, but I felt it. That sense of

So my garage has become one of those situations where you can't actually park a car in it anymore. I think it happened gradually—you know how it goes. First it's just a few boxes from when you moved, then some old paint cans, then your partner's exercise equipment they swore they'd use, then somehow there's a broken ladder leaning against the wall and a shelf unit that's missing three shelves.

Last weekend I finally did something about it. Not because I'm particularly organized—I'm really not—but because I needed to actually use the space again. Found a screwdriver I've been looking for for two years. It was in a plastic tub under a deflated beach ball.

The first thing I did was just pull everything out into the driveway. This took longer than expected. You find stuff you'd completely forgotten existed. A box of old cassette tapes. A camping lantern with no fuel. Several mismatched pairs of work gloves. My neighbor Ruth came by and made a comment about me having a yard sale, which was funny because yeah, it basically looked like that.

Then I had to make actual decisions. Throw away, keep, donate. It sounds simple but it takes longer than you'd think. I kept second-guessing myself on things. Do I really need to keep these old wooden crates? Probably not, but what if they're useful someday? That's the trap, obviously. Usefulness in some theoretical future versus the actual space in your garage right now.

I ended up with three piles. The donate pile got pretty big. Turns out you don't need five extension cords. I brought the larger items to a place that takes that stuff. The throw-away pile was mostly broken things that weren't worth fixing.

For what I kept, I tried to be more intentional about where it went. Tools on one wall. Seasonal stuff in labeled boxes on a shelf. Garden equipment in one corner. It's not perfect. There's still a box of things I couldn't categorize sitting in the back, and I've got a feeling it'll be there for a while.

But I can actually pull my car in now, which feels like a small victory. It probably won't stay this way forever. That's just how these spaces work. But at least for right now it's functional, and I found that screwdriver, so I'm calling it a success.

The Garage Reorganisation Project

I spent most of Saturday moving things around in the garage. Not in any systematic way at first—just shifting boxes from one corner to another, creating new piles that seemed to make more sense than the old ones. By afternoon I'd moved everything at least twice and had made minimal progress.

The problem with a garage that's been used for storage is that you lose track of what's actually in it. There were three boxes of books stacked behind the bicycles that I'd completely forgotten about. A collection of plant pots, most of them cracked. Two sets of shelving units that were leaning against the back wall, partially obscured by a rolled-up carpet and some lengths of timber. The timber had been there since we did some work on the deck three years ago.

What helped eventually was moving backwards. Instead of trying to organise everything into categories, I started by removing the items I knew I wouldn't use again. The broken pots went into bags. The timber got loaded onto a trailer. I found old paint tins that had solidified and a stack of plastic storage boxes that had warped in the heat. Once those were cleared, I had actual space to work with.

Noor came out around three o'clock to check on progress and pointed out that I'd been shuffling the same tools around without actually sorting them. She was right. I had three hammers, four screwdrivers without handles, a collection of mismatched nails in various containers. We spent the next hour grouping things properly—all the small hand tools went into one toolbox, the larger power tools onto a dedicated shelf, fasteners into a single plastic organiser.

The garage still isn't finished. There are boxes in the corner that I haven't looked through yet, and Ruth's camping equipment is spread across the floor waiting to be assessed. The bicycles still need to be mounted on the wall properly instead of just leaning there. But at least now there's a clear distinction between the things we use regularly and the things we're keeping for unclear reasons.

The main thing I've realised is that you can't organise a space while it's still full of everything. You have to reduce first, then arrange. The temptation is to find clever storage solutions for things you don't actually need, when the real solution is simply not keeping them.

The wheelie bins are full now. Next weekend I'll probably tackle the boxes that are still sitting there unopened.

Spent the better part of two hours today wrestling with the philodendron, which has apparently decided it's rooted itself into that plastic pot with the commitment of a medieval peasant to their lord. I'd been meaning to do this for months—the soil's been compacted into something resembling old felt, and water just runs straight through the sides now without actually reaching the roots. The plant itself looks fine, which is infuriating. Lush, almost smug about it.

I gathered everything first, which felt virtuous. New pot, slightly larger. Fresh soil. A trowel. Newspaper spread across the kitchen floor because I learned that lesson the hard way years ago with the snake plant incident. But nothing—and I mean nothing—prepared me for the actual extraction. The root ball had fused with the pot somehow. Physics I don't understand. I ran the pot under water, tried gently working around the edges with a knife, even held it upside down and gave it a light tap. The plant simply refused.

At one point I actually considered just breaking the pot, and I would have, except it's reasonably nice and I'm apparently sentimental about plastic containers now, which says something about my life I'd rather not examine too closely.

Eventually it came loose—suddenly, anticlimactically, after I'd stopped trying so hard. Of course. There was a moment of panic when I first pulled it free because the roots had this greyish tint I wasn't expecting, but they smelled fine and looked healthy enough when I loosened them with my fingers. The old soil fell away in these satisfying chunks.

The new pot felt absurdly roomy. I kept second-guessing whether I'd chosen something too large, whether I'd read about the rules correctly—something about an inch and a half clearance on all sides? I don't actually know. I've never looked this up. I just fill the pot with soil until it feels approximately right and hope for the best.

Now it's sitting by the window looking exactly as it did before, which is both disappointing and exactly what you'd expect. It'll probably thrive now. They always do, right after you've given them the attention they were apparently dying without. I'll water it once more in a few days, or maybe tomorrow. I'll probably forget and do it next week.

At least my hands smell like earth.

Spent most of the afternoon dealing with the rubber plant in the living room. It's been at least eighteen months since the last repotting, maybe longer—I should really keep better records of these things. The root ball had become severely compacted, the soil was draining poorly, and I could see roots circling around the base when I tipped the pot. All the standard indicators that it needed moving up a size.

The difficulty was getting the plant out of the pot in the first place. I'd watered it lightly the night before, thinking that might loosen things, but the roots had adhered so firmly to the interior that I had to run a knife around the perimeter and flex the ceramic pot repeatedly. Even then, when it finally came free, I had to support the base of the plant with one hand to prevent the entire root system from tearing. There's always that moment of uncertainty where you're applying force and not entirely sure if you're about to damage something irreversible.

Once extracted, the root ball was much more densely packed than I'd anticipated. The outer two or three inches were completely root-bound—just white fibers with minimal soil visible. I used my fingers to gently separate and loosen the outer sections, being careful not to tear the fine root hairs. The inner portion was still fairly intact, which was good. I removed what I could of the old compacted medium, probably extracted about forty percent of the original soil. Some sources suggest being more aggressive with this, but I find the plant responds better to a gentler approach.

Prepared a new potting mixture—standard ratio, nothing exotic. Let it sit and hydrate for a few minutes before use. The new pot is only marginally larger than the previous one, about two centimeters in diameter. Larger pots retain more moisture, and this particular plant doesn't seem to appreciate that. Better to err on the conservative side.

Positioned the plant at approximately the same depth as before, maybe imperceptibly deeper, and back-filled with the new medium. The whole operation took roughly forty minutes from start to finish. I pressed the soil down around the base to eliminate obvious air pockets but tried not to compress it excessively.

Watered it thoroughly immediately after repotting—that's standard practice, helps settle the medium and reduces transplant shock. The drainage was noticeably better than before; the water ran through cleanly without pooling at the surface.

Left it in its usual spot with indirect light. I'll monitor the soil moisture over the next week or so. Usually there's a brief period of adjustment where the plant appears slightly stressed before settling into the new pot. Sometimes the leaves develop a subtle dullness for a few days. It should bounce back within ten to fourteen days if everything goes as expected.

The whole process was unremarkable, really, but necessary. The plant was clearly struggling within its previous constraints. Should have done this sooner.

How to Fix a Squeaky Bicycle

That squeaking sound your bike makes when you pedal is annoying, and it's one of the easiest problems to actually solve yourself. You don't need special tools or any real mechanical knowledge. Most of the time it takes five minutes.

The first thing to do is figure out where the noise is coming from. Ride the bike slowly and listen carefully. Is it happening when you pedal, or is it more of a rhythmic squeak that matches your wheel rotation? This matters because the fix is different depending on the source.

If the squeak happens with every pedal stroke, it's almost certainly your seatpost. The post that holds your seat in the frame gets dry, and metal rubbing on metal creates that high-pitched sound. The solution is straightforward: take your seat off (usually just an Allen key), apply some lubricant to the metal post—any general purpose bike lube or even a little silicone spray—then slide it back in and tighten. Wipe off any excess. Done.

Another common culprit is the pedal itself. If you notice the squeak really intensifies when you're pushing hard up a hill, try this: rotate each pedal to see if it's moving freely in the crank arm. Sometimes they work loose over time. If a pedal feels even slightly stiff, remove it, clean out any dirt from the threads using an old rag, apply a thin coat of lube, and screw it back in firmly. They should spin smoothly.

For wheel-related squeaking—where the noise corresponds to the wheel turning rather than your pedaling—the issue is usually brake pads that have glazed over or collected grime. This is trickier to fix properly, but you can start by cleaning the brake pads with fine sandpaper and cleaning the rim surface where the pads make contact. Sometimes this alone stops the noise. If it doesn't, the pads might genuinely be worn down and need replacing, which is a bit more involved.

The chain can squeak too, though it's less common. If you suspect this, clean the chain thoroughly with an old brush and some degreaser, dry it completely, then apply fresh chain lube. Wipe away the excess.

One thing people often overlook: sometimes the squeak isn't really coming from the bike itself but from the cage that holds your water bottle, or from a reflector or light that's come loose. Grab different parts of your bike while it's sitting still and move them gently. If anything shifts or flexes, tighten it.

The real key to preventing squeaks is basic maintenance. Keep your bike dry, wipe it down after wet rides, and lube the moving parts occasionally. A bike that's looked after rarely makes annoying noises. Priya mentioned once that she just rides hers until something sounds wrong, and I think most people are like that. But catching these things early—like when it's just beginning to squeak—means you can fix it in minutes rather than ignoring it until you're really frustrated.

Subject: why do i keep doing this to myself on weekday mornings

so i decided yesterday that today would be the day i finally made proper bread instead of just buying it like a normal person. had that whole thing where you read one article about how simple it is and suddenly you're convinced you've been living wrong.

woke up at 6:30 thinking i had plenty of time. mixed the dough by 7 which felt very industrious and responsible. the recipe said "let rise for an hour." fine. i can work with that. except apparently that was optimistic because at 7 hours later it's still... doing something. just sitting there. growing. getting more aggressive about its existence.

the problem is i'm not actually sure what risen dough is supposed to look like when it's done rising. the description "roughly double in size" is somehow both completely clear and entirely useless. is it double? is it 1.7x? i genuinely cannot tell. it looks puffy, which seems like a reasonable outcome given that i've been waiting this long. but there's always that voice in the back of your head going "what if this is actually overproofed and you're about to bake a brick."

anyway i've now spent the entire morning staring at this bowl. made coffee three times. took a shower. did some laundry. came back to look at the bowl again. it hasn't changed noticeably in the last twenty minutes so presumably it's done? or maybe it peaked forty minutes ago and it's been downhill since.

there's also the question of what happens next. the recipe has a step called "shaping" which is apparently distinct from "just putting it in a pan" but the instructions are vague in that specific way that suggests everyone else already knows what this means. Beatriz made bread once and when i asked her she said "you just shape it" which is technically correct information delivered in the least helpful possible way.

i've got the dutch oven preheating which is making my kitchen aggressively hot. genuinely considering just calling this whole thing off and getting a croissant from somewhere, but i've already invested this much time and i'd like to not be the kind of person who gives up on bread dough at 1 PM on a thursday.

the worst part is that even if this somehow works and comes out looking like actual bread, i still have to wait for it to cool before eating it. so the entire process is essentially "commit 4-5 hours of your day to a thing you could have bought in five minutes." and yet here we are. that's the commit i've made.

will update later if this doesn't just turn into a dense, gluey disappointment.

Repotting a Stubborn Houseplant

When a houseplant resists removal from its current pot—roots bound tightly against the walls, soil compacted and seemingly fused to the container—the process requires patience and a methodical approach. The following steps outline a reliable technique for extracting and repotting plants that have become difficult to manage.

**Assessment and Preparation**

Before attempting removal, water the plant thoroughly, but not to the point of saturation. A moderately moist soil mass will slide more easily from the pot than either bone-dry or waterlogged substrate. Allow the water to settle for fifteen to twenty minutes. Gather your materials: a new pot (ideally one to two inches larger in diameter), fresh potting medium appropriate to the plant species, newspaper or a drop cloth, a wooden dowel or chopstick, and a small knife or root hook if available.

**Initial Extraction**

Lay the plant on its side on your work surface. Gently apply pressure to the pot's exterior, rolling it slightly to help break the seal between soil and container walls. This action loosens compacted material without shocking the root system. If the pot is ceramic or rigid plastic, this rolling motion is crucial; flexible pots can be squeezed gently to create separation.

Run a knife blade around the interior perimeter of the pot if the plant still won't budge. Keep the blade angled toward the pot wall rather than toward the roots to avoid damage. Work methodically around the entire circumference. Do not rush this step, as forcing removal can snap the main stem or tear critical roots.

**Extracting Stubborn Root Balls**

For plants that remain stuck, invert the pot and tap its bottom firmly but not violently against a sturdy surface. Support the plant's base with your free hand. Sometimes several taps are necessary. If this fails, running warm water over the pot's exterior may help, as it can slightly soften some soils and expand the container material just enough to release tension.

As a last resort, you may need to sacrifice the pot. Ceramic pots can be carefully cracked with a hammer or utility knife. Plastic containers can be cut away using tin snips or a utility knife. Once the root ball emerges, trim any roots that are circling tightly or appear damaged.

**Repotting Process**

Place fresh potting medium in the new container to a depth of approximately two inches. Position the plant so that the soil line on the stem aligns with the pot's rim. Fill around the sides with additional medium, pressing gently as you work. Do not compact aggressively; the soil should be firm enough to support the plant but loose enough for water and air penetration. Water thoroughly after repotting to settle the medium.

For the first week following repotting, reduce watering slightly and maintain the plant in moderate indirect light. This allows the root system to recover from the disturbance and establish itself in the new substrate. A stubborn plant may show temporary wilting or leaf drop; this is normal and typically resolves within a few days as roots acclimate.

Three Ways to Brew Coffee

Most mornings I'm just trying to get caffeine into my body before 8 a.m., but there are three methods I rotate through depending on how much time I have and what kind of mood I'm in. None of these require special equipment or training, which is the point.

The first is the pour-over. You need a filter cone, filters, and about four minutes. I use a mug with a filter cone sitting on top, hot water just off boiling, and medium-ground coffee. The ratio is roughly two tablespoons per eight ounces of water, though honestly I don't measure anymore. You pour a little water first to let the grounds bloom—this takes about thirty seconds and apparently matters—then pour the rest slowly in circles. It's meditative if you're that type of person. It's annoying if you're not. The coffee tastes clean, which is nice. The ritual is nice too, I guess.

The second is the French press, which takes longer but requires less attention. You add coarse grounds and hot water, stir it once, then wait four minutes. Meanwhile you can shower or check your email or just stand there. When the timer goes off, you press the plunger down slowly—if you go too fast the grounds shoot up and make a mess—and pour into your mug. This method leaves some sediment at the bottom, which some people hate. I don't mind it. The coffee tastes heavier, richer, which is why I use this one when I want to actually taste what I'm drinking instead of just waking up.

The third is the simplest: just hot water and grounds in a mug. Let them settle for a few minutes, then drink carefully so you don't get a mouthful of powder. People call this cowboy coffee or mud, and it's what you do when you're camping or rushed or both. It's not elegant. The grounds get everywhere. But it's fast and it works, and sometimes that's all that matters.

Felix swears by the pour-over because he likes the control. Marisol uses the press because she makes it for two people at once. I bounce between all three depending on the day. Wednesday mornings I have time to mess with the pour-over. Thursday I'm already running late. Saturday morning when nobody needs me until noon, the press sits on the kitchen counter for twenty minutes while I read something pointless on my phone.

The honest truth is they're all just hot water filtering through coffee grounds. The differences are real—the flavor profile actually does change—but the differences are also kind of small. What matters more is that you have some ritual, some moment that's yours before the day starts pulling at you. Whether that takes two minutes or five minutes depends on what you've got that morning.

The coffee itself is almost secondary to the fact of making it.

So I finally started tackling my garage last weekend and I'm only now remembering why I put it off for like two years. It's genuinely a disaster in there. My partner keeps saying we should just rent a skip and be done with it but there's stuff in there that's actually useful, it's just buried under about six layers of other stuff that probably isn't.

The problem is that we moved in and instead of actually unpacking boxes and finding homes for things, we just kind of... shoved it all through the garage door and closed it. Which works fine until it doesn't, and suddenly you can't even get the car in properly and you're parking on the drive like animals.

I started by just pulling everything out onto the driveway which took a whole morning. I didn't realise how much there was. There's the usual suspects — broken garden chairs, old paint cans that are probably dried up, a ladder that's missing a rung, some boxes of books that should've gone to the charity shop years ago. But then there's random stuff like three different sets of Christmas lights (though I'm pretty sure only one of them works), a fish tank that nobody uses, and honestly I have no idea where half of it came from.

Marisol from next door came over and was like "why are you keeping all this" and I was like "I don't know, you never know when you might need something?" which is obviously ridiculous but also kind of true sometimes. Like I found the old camping equipment buried under some storage boxes and actually we could use that this summer probably.

The hardest part is deciding what to actually keep versus what to throw away or donate. I've got piles everywhere — one pile for things that are actually broken beyond repair, one pile of stuff that works but we don't use, and then this middle pile of things that I'm not sure about yet. That middle pile keeps getting bigger. My partner says that's procrastination but I think it's just being realistic about decision-making.

I've managed to actually get the shelving unit cleared off and I'm planning to put that to better use. If I organize things vertically instead of just stacking boxes on top of each other, it might actually work. I found some plastic storage containers at the back that I'd forgotten about, so I reckon I can use those for things like extension cords and smaller tools that are currently just rolling around loose.

The thing is I genuinely don't hate it? Like it's horrible and tedious and my back is killing me, but there's something weirdly satisfying about creating piles and actually being able to see the floor. And I can park the car in there now which is brilliant. Still got probably another day's work to do to actually get it organized properly but at least the chaos is visible instead of just this oppressive wall of stuff when you open the door.

Anyway if anyone has tips on storage systems that don't cost a fortune I'm open to suggestions because I'm determined to keep this actually organized once it's done.

The ficus has been in the same pot for three years. I know this because I remember buying it at the grocery store during that phase when everything felt manageable, before the leaks in the kitchen and before things got complicated with my mother. The pot is ceramic, terracotta-colored, with a single drainage hole that stopped draining properly sometime last year. Now when I water it, the soil stays wet. The leaves have started doing that thing where they yellow from the inside out, and I keep telling myself I'll fix it next weekend.

Today is the weekend, so here I am with a larger pot, fresh soil, and the plant sitting on newspapers spread across the kitchen table like I'm performing surgery.

The roots, when I finally manage to loosen the root ball, are worse than I expected. They're matted and pale, circling the bottom in the compressed spiral of something that's been desperate for space. The plant has been slowly strangling itself, and I've been watering it more frequently, which only made everything worse. This is the kind of thing you can read about online, but understanding it intellectually and understanding it while you're holding a root-bound plant are two different things. I feel vaguely guilty, the way you do when you realize you've been trying to help something and actually made it worse.

Getting the plant out of the pot takes ten minutes. I have to run a knife around the inside edge, tip it sideways, tap the bottom, coax it. The ceramic doesn't want to let go. At one point I consider just breaking the pot, which would be easier, but I'd rather not make more work.

Once it's out, I do what the articles suggest: I score the root ball gently with my fingernail, trying to loosen it. The soil crumbles. Some roots separate, which feels significant. I pick out the compacted dirt from the bottom and sides, working carefully because I don't want to damage the fine root hairs, though I suspect most of them are already dead. The whole operation is slower and more tedious than I anticipated.

The new pot is four inches wider. I add soil to the bottom, position the ficus so the top of the old root ball sits about an inch below the rim, and pack fresh soil around the sides. I water it lightly, not thoroughly, because wet roots are what got us here in the first place. The leaves still look tired, but the plant is standing straighter, somehow. It's probably wishful thinking.

I'll need to be more careful about watering. Let it dry out a bit between waterings, the articles said. I know myself well enough to know I'll probably still overwater it, but maybe less than before. Maybe I've learned something from three years of slow neglect disguised as care.

The plant is back on the windowsill. It looks the same, mostly. In a few weeks I'll know if I've actually fixed anything, or just prolonged the problem. For now, I'm leaving the empty pot in the sink. I'll deal with cleaning it tomorrow.

There's something about a rainy afternoon that makes a board game feel necessary rather than frivolous. The kind of rain that's settled in for the day—not dramatic, just steady and gray—leaves you with the feeling that going outside would be pointless, so you might as well commit to staying in.

We had the board games stacked in the cupboard where they've been for months. Felix pulled them out one by one, reading the boxes like he was considering dinner options. The ones in the back were slightly warped from humidity. Scrabble with a bent corner. That word game nobody quite remembers the rules for anymore. The strategy one that takes three hours and always causes some kind of argument about whether a particular move is unfair.

We went with something simpler. Something that wouldn't require us to consult the rulebook halfway through or stop to have a discussion about game mechanics. The kind where you mostly understand what's happening and the afternoon can unfold without too much friction.

It's strange how a game creates its own rhythm. You settle into it. The first round feels slow because nobody's quite invested yet, then something shifts. Someone gets ahead, someone else complains it's rigged, someone makes an unlikely comeback. Beatriz knocked over her water glass reaching for the dice, which caused the brief chaos that every game needs—the scramble to save pieces, the paper towels, the joking about it being a sign. Then back to playing.

The rain kept doing its thing outside. You could hear it on the windows, a constant soft percussion that made the inside feel warmer and more enclosed. The kitchen where we were playing gradually filled with that particular afternoon dimness that comes early on those days. Someone turned on a lamp. We kept playing.

What I noticed, or what struck me anyway, was how little anyone checked their phone.
