There's often a pull toward that—a notification, a thought about something you need to do—but with the game going, the momentum of it, people just stayed present. Felix got competitive about a run of good luck. Beatriz played slowly, considering each move like it mattered. We laughed at small things. Someone made a strategic error and immediately regretted it.

By the time we stopped, the light outside had shifted. The rain was still there but the afternoon was moving toward evening. We hadn't accomplished anything. The house was still as messy as it was in the morning. Nothing had been solved or finished or improved. We'd just sat at a table and moved pieces around for a couple of hours and occasionally disputed the rules.

But there was something restorative about it anyway, the way those afternoons are. Not in a productive sense, but in some other way that's harder to name. The game had given us a reason to sit together without it feeling planned or effortful. The rain had given us permission to stop doing other things.

# Getting the Garage Sorted

The garage has become a problem. Not the slow kind of problem where you notice it gradually—the kind where you open the side door one morning and just stand there, looking at the heap of things, and think: this needs to happen today.

Mine got like that over about eighteen months. The usual way: things that didn't fit indoors went out here. The exercise bike that nobody uses. Three cardboard boxes of old paperwork. A broken garden chair. Rolls of garden netting. Paint tins from jobs that finished two years ago. The shelving unit that collapsed on itself. Several bags of clothes marked for the charity shop that never quite made it into the car. You know the type of garage.

The trick is to actually commit to doing it, because a half-done garage is worse than a full one. That's the main thing I've learned. You need a day when you're genuinely prepared to move everything out onto the driveway and the lawn, and you need to be alright with the house looking chaotic for six or seven hours.

I started by pulling everything out. It's the only way to see what you've actually got. Once it's spread across the concrete outside, you get that awful honest reckoning: four flat sheets of plywood you'll never use. Two broken lamps. A collection of plant pots. Eight tins of paint in colours you forgot you owned. Thao helped me do mine and kept saying we should have done this ages ago, which was true and also unhelpful.

The hard part isn't the throwing away—it's the decision-making. A lot of what you pull out isn't obviously rubbish. The exercise bike works fine, but is anyone actually going to use it? The garden chair is broken but in theory could be mended. The paint might be useful for touching up. You have to make actual decisions instead of just leaving things in limbo.

What helped was making proper piles. Not two piles—actual piles. Things to throw. Things to donate. Things to try to sell. Things worth fixing. Things that genuinely belong in the garage and will be kept. This took longer than expected because I was frequently wrong about which pile things should go in.

The selling bit is minimal effort if you're realistic. Take a photo, post it, and if someone wants it in three days they can have it, otherwise it goes. Don't hold onto things for months waiting for that perfect buyer.

Once the piles were done, everything went back in but differently. Tools in one area. Seasonal things at the back. Smaller equipment on the shelving—the useful shelving, not the collapsed one. Boxes neatly stacked instead of scattered. Bikes hung up on the wall. The garden stuff corralled together. Suddenly you can actually walk through it and find things.

It's been about three weeks now and it's stayed reasonably organized. Not perfect—there's a pile of newspapers building up again and I know someone's left a bag of something-or-other in the corner. But it's manageable. You can get the car in without moving things. That's the standard really.

The thing about coffee is that you end up thinking about it more than seems reasonable for a beverage that tastes mostly like bitterness and regret. Yet here I am, rotating through three methods like some kind of amateur ritual specialist, each one promising something different and delivering approximately the same result: a cup of dark liquid that wakes you up.

The pour-over is what I reach for on mornings when I have time and want to feel like I'm doing something intentional. There's a meditative quality to it, or that's what I tell myself while heating water to exactly the right temperature and pouring it in slow circles. The filter paper does something noble and important-seeming. Everything takes about four minutes, during which I can convince myself I'm not just standing in my kitchen waiting for caffeine. The coffee tastes clean. Sometimes I think about how my mother did this exact thing for thirty years, and whether she also felt like she was performing competence for an invisible audience.

The French press sits on the counter accumulating a thin ring of oils and sediment. It's honest in a way that feels almost aggressive—no filters pretending to improve things, just ground coffee and hot water in direct contact. You wait four minutes, push the plunger down with mild resistance, and pour. There are always fine particles at the bottom of the cup, a reminder that nothing is truly separated. The coffee tastes heavier, more itself. It's what I use when I don't care about seeming refined, which is most days, but I don't use it most days because the cleanup feels slightly worse than the taste is better. This is the calculus of a tired person.

The instant is what actually happens most often, though it feels like a confession to admit it. You pour hot water into a mug with a spoonful of powder that cost less than either of the other methods and dissolves immediately. It tastes like the idea of coffee, which is to say it tastes like nothing much, like disappointment that you've made peace with. My friend Ruth claims she prefers it, that all the fussing over temperature and grind size is nonsense, and maybe she's right or maybe she's just accepted her circumstances, which might be the same thing.

What's strangest is that the coffee from all three methods goes into my body and does the same job. The biological end point is identical. Yet I spend actual time thinking about which method suits my morning, as if the coffee itself cares about the philosophical approach. As if the brewing method is a reflection of who I am or want to be on any given Tuesday.

Probably most people don't think about this. Probably they just drink coffee and get on with their day without noticing they've appointed themselves a minor curator of their own minor rituals. But once you start noticing which vessel and method you're reaching for, you can't really stop. It's just coffee, and also it's the entire texture of how you move through a morning, and there's no way to separate the two things, so you keep rotating through your three methods and pretending the choice matters, which it doesn't, which is why it

How to Manage a Rainy-Day Board-Game Afternoon

The logistics of an afternoon indoors with board games require some basic preparation, particularly when multiple players are involved and weather conditions have already limited other options.

Begin by selecting an appropriate space. The dining table works well if the surface is clear and level; a coffee table can work for smaller games but tends to create awkward reaching distances. Ensure adequate lighting from overhead sources or lamps positioned to avoid glare on the board itself. Natural light from windows becomes unreliable during rain, so artificial lighting should be your primary consideration.

Gather the necessary materials before players arrive. Check that all game components are present—dice, tokens, cards, instruction booklets. Missing pieces derail sessions quickly. Set out water glasses and consider snacks that won't leave residue on cards or game boards. Nuts, crackers, or fruit work better than chocolate or anything with sticky coating.

When players arrive, clarify game selection early. If playing with mixed experience levels, choose something accessible to newer players rather than complex strategy games that require extensive rule explanation. A thirty-minute explanation of turn structure consumes enthusiasm. Games that accommodate variable player counts provide flexibility if someone needs to leave or additional people arrive.

Before beginning, assign someone to read the rules aloud while others follow along with the instruction booklet. This takes longer initially but prevents mid-game disputes about mechanics. Have one person serve as rules arbiter to maintain consistency.

Establish turn order and basic expectations about pacing. Some players move quickly; others deliberate extensively. Gentle time suggestions help—"take about two minutes per turn" works better than letting one person spend ten minutes on a single decision. Keep the atmosphere collaborative rather than competitive, especially with casual afternoon games.

Manage the physical game space throughout play. Keep the board centered and protected from accidental spills. Track game state clearly so confusion doesn't build. If playing multiple games, keep the first game's components separated from the second to avoid mixing pieces.

Plan for natural breaks between games. People need to stretch, refill drinks, use bathrooms. These pauses prevent fatigue and argument-creep. A rainy afternoon accommodates multiple shorter games better than one exhausting marathon session.

As the afternoon progresses and energy drops, transition toward simpler games or wind down rather than introducing complex new ones. The goal shifts from entertainment to maintaining the mood. By late afternoon, conversation matters more than competition. Consider this final phase part of the session rather than an afterthought.
